divorce-cost.description: Estimate the total cost of a divorce including filing fees, attorney hours, mediation, and court costs — for both contested and uncontested cases.
divorce-cost.shortDescription: Estimate the total cost of a divorce.

faqs.question: What's the average cost of divorce?
faqs.answer: Uncontested: $500–$2,500. Contested: $15,000–$30,000+ per spouse. Costs scale with assets, custody disputes, and trial length.

faqs.question: Can I avoid attorney fees?
faqs.answer: Yes — DIY filings work for simple, uncontested divorces with no children or shared assets. Most courts have self-help packets.

faqs.question: Does the loser pay fees?
faqs.answer: Rarely. Most US courts require each spouse to pay their own attorney unless one party acted in bad faith.

sol-deadline.name: Statute of Limitations Deadline Calculator
sol-deadline.description: Enter the date your claim arose and your state — get the exact filing deadline and how many days you have left to sue. Covers contracts, personal injury, fraud, and property damage.
sol-deadline.shortDescription: Find your exact filing deadline by date.

faqs.question: What if my deadline already passed?
faqs.answer: Your claim is likely time-barred. Some narrow exceptions (discovery rule, fraudulent concealment, minor plaintiffs) may apply — talk to a lawyer immediately.

faqs.question: When does the clock start?
faqs.answer: Usually on the date of the injury, breach, or discovery of harm — depending on the claim and state.

faqs.question: Can the deadline be paused?
faqs.answer: Yes — 'tolling' can pause the clock for minors, mental incapacity, or defendant absence from the state.

wrongful-term-value.name: Wrongful Termination Case Value Estimator
wrongful-term-value.description: Estimate the potential value of a wrongful termination claim including lost wages, lost benefits, emotional distress damages, and attorney fees.
wrongful-term-value.shortDescription: Estimate your wrongful termination case value.

faqs.question: What damages are typical?
faqs.answer: Back pay (wages from termination to settlement), front pay (future lost earnings), benefits, and in some cases emotional distress and punitive damages.

faqs.question: Do I have to mitigate damages?
faqs.answer: Yes — you must make reasonable efforts to find new work. Wages from a new job offset back pay.

faqs.question: What's a typical settlement?
faqs.answer: Most wrongful termination cases settle for 6–12 months of salary. High-profile or discrimination cases can reach $500K+.

severance-fairness.name: Severance Offer Fairness Score
severance-fairness.description: Score your severance offer 0–100 against industry benchmarks for your role, tenure, and salary level. Get a fairness rating and negotiation guidance.
severance-fairness.shortDescription: Score your severance offer against benchmarks.

faqs.question: Is severance required by law?
faqs.answer: No — most US employers are not required to offer severance unless promised in a contract or policy. Many do as goodwill or in exchange for a release of claims.

faqs.question: Should I negotiate?
faqs.answer: Almost always yes. Initial offers are typically 20–40% below what employers will pay if pushed, especially for tenured or senior staff.

faqs.question: What should I look for?
faqs.answer: Severance amount, healthcare continuation, outplacement services, non-disparagement balance, and the scope of the release of claims.

lease-break-penalty.name: Lease Break / Early Termination Penalty Calculator
lease-break-penalty.description: Estimate the financial penalty for breaking your lease early — including months remaining, mitigation by your landlord, and state-specific caps on liability.
lease-break-penalty.shortDescription: Estimate the cost of breaking your lease.

faqs.question: Can I just walk away?
faqs.answer: Not without consequences. Most leases let landlords sue for unpaid rent through the end of the term, plus advertising and re-rental costs.

faqs.question: Does my landlord have to find a new tenant?
faqs.answer: In most states, yes — landlords have a 'duty to mitigate' by trying to re-rent the unit. Once re-rented, your liability ends.

faqs.question: Are there legal reasons to break a lease?
faqs.answer: Active military duty (SCRA), domestic violence (state-specific), uninhabitable conditions, and landlord harassment are common protected reasons.
